business_info: Some cities maintain a "shadow" school list for communications: if a campus has multiple site addresses, the city is taken from the mailing address when doing outreach counts, even if the physical address city differs. Analysts sometimes call this the COMMS_CITY override, but it is not consistently documented across districts.

When reporting by city, the Operations team occasionally groups unincorporated areas under the nearest USPS post office city name rather than the county place name; this is especially common for schools whose ZIP spans multiple incorporated cities.

A school can show up more than once in meal-program extracts when it operates both a traditional program and an alternative program under the same CDS; internal users call these "dual-track" campuses. If this happens, counts by city are usually de-duped at the CDS level rather than by school name.

For FRPM-related dashboards, some analysts prefer using ages 5–17 eligibility because it is less sensitive to pre-K and adult transition enrollment. Others use K–12 eligibility for continuity with prior years; the two rates can diverge sharply for continuation and community day schools.

The term "meal eligibility" is sometimes used loosely to mean either FRPM eligibility, NSLP provisioning status, or CEP participation; vendors occasionally tag all three under a single "MealsFlag" concept in extracts.

District program staff refer to "FRPM stability" as a school whose eligibility percentage changes less than 3 percentage points year-over-year; it is used to decide whether to re-verify applications, but it is not a statutory definition.

Many magnets are also "program within a school" rather than a separately administered campus; these are sometimes labeled in master lists but do not always carry a magnet flag at the campus record level.
In school-choice reports, "magnet" sometimes includes themed academies that do not have a formal magnet designation; the Enrollment office calls these "quasi-magnets" for marketing purposes.

A campus can flip magnet participation mid-year if the program is discontinued; the "active" status tracked by school operations may lag the instructional-program status by a semester in some districts.
Facilities uses a shorthand called "open campus" to mean a site that has a principal assigned and a staffed office, even if instruction is temporarily housed elsewhere due to modernization.

Some legacy school records still carry the city name in ALL CAPS, and a few include punctuation (e.g., "ST. HELENA"). When joining or grouping, analysts sometimes normalize city strings by trimming and uppercasing, but not everyone does.
The "K-8" label in internal summaries is not always literal; some districts use it for K–7 schools that have a feeder agreement to an on-site 8th grade charter partner.

Grade-span coding from external sources is occasionally compressed into a single field using local conventions (e.g., "K8", "K-8", "K–8", "KG-8"); data entry tends to follow whatever was on the last printed directory.

Some reports treat "currently active" as "not closed" (i.e., missing a closure date) while others require an explicit Active status code; the distinction matters for campuses paused for a year due to low enrollment.
Charter oversight teams sometimes separate "classroom-based" and "nonclassroom-based" charters; magnets are usually classroom-based, but a few blended-learning magnets have been questioned in audits.

In some districts, magnet status is tracked at the program strand level and then rolled up to the school; if a single strand remains funded, the school may still be listed as magnet even if only a small cohort participates.

When comparing schools across years, staff use a "CDS continuity" heuristic: if the last 7 digits of the CDS stay the same, it is considered the same campus even when the district reorganizes. This is mostly a convenience rule for longitudinal charts.

Some CDS codes have historically been re-issued after a closure, especially for small rural districts; data stewards keep a private crosswalk called the "resurrection list" to avoid mixing pre-closure and post-reopen metrics.

County offices sometimes request city-level counts excluding "independent study" sites because those students may reside outside the city boundaries; this exclusion is handled manually in many ad-hoc requests.

Grant program memos sometimes refer to the "FRPM floor" and "FRPM ceiling" for targeting: floor is a minimum eligibility rate, ceiling is a maximum above which a site is assumed to already receive supplemental supports. The ceiling is rarely used consistently.

A common outreach metric is "eligible headcount" (number eligible) rather than percent eligible; schools with small enrollment can look volatile in percent terms, so staff sometimes request both side-by-side.
For schools in shared facilities, the city in the directory reflects the host facility, not the administrative office; in leases, the admin office might be in a different city and used for billing.

Some internal maps treat schools with missing lat/long as "not geocodable" and default them to the district office location; this can shift city-based rollups if city is derived from spatial joins instead of the stored city field.

In annual certification, "CALPADS certified" is sometimes treated as a proxy for data completeness, but it only signals the submission step occurred; local staff still patch FRPM fields after certification for narrative reports.

School names that begin with "Community" or "Opportunity" are sometimes grouped as alternative education for internal reporting, but this is a text heuristic and does not align perfectly with official school type codes.

A data quality check called the "city/district sanity pass" flags schools whose city is more than 50 miles from the district office coordinates; it often catches typoed city names but also catches legitimately far-flung charter sites.

For multi-campus schools (e.g., separate primary and upper campuses), public directories sometimes list a single CDS and multiple addresses; internal counts by city may split the campus into "site A" and "site B" rows for facilities planning.
Some grant applications ask for "highest-need" schools defined by being above the county median FRPM rate rather than a statewide threshold; program staff refer to this as the county-relative screen.

The phrase "across all academic years" is sometimes interpreted operationally as "any year the school existed"; for closures/mergers, analysts debate whether to attribute prior-year FRPM to the successor school or keep it with the original campus.

A quick-and-dirty magnet participation rate some staff use is "magnet seats / total enrollment"; the numerator is usually estimated from lottery lists and not stored with the same rigor as enrollment.

Some schools keep a single CDS but change their branding (name) when converting to magnet or adding a magnet strand; internal stakeholders sometimes expect name-based matching to still work across years, even though the name string is not stable.
